common_name: Sausage Tree (African Sausage Tree, Kigelia, Fetish Tree)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in late spring in full sun with well-drained soil; Kigelia africana is frost-sensitive and best suited to zone 10-11 for outdoor planting, or zone 9 in containers. The tree grows rapidly in tropical conditions. The enormous, pendulous fruits (1–2 feet long, up to 20 lbs) hang from long, ropy, cable-like stalks from the branches and must not be allowed to fall on people or parked vehicles — site accordingly. The fruits are NOT edible fresh and are considered toxic. Night-blooming red flowers require bat pollination for fruit set.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil — Sausage Tree grows most vigorously in full sun with adequate water during establishment, becoming drought-tolerant in deep, well-drained soils once established. Kigelia africana is a large, wide-spreading, semi-evergreen tree native to tropical Africa (from Sudan and Ethiopia south to Namibia and South Africa) in the Bignoniaceae family (the trumpet vine family), widely known for producing one of the most extraordinary fruits of any tree. The fruits are enormous, cylindrical, sausage-shaped, hard-shelled, fibrous, gray-brown, and can reach 2 feet in length and 20 pounds in weight — each hanging individually from long, ropy, cable-like stalks attached to the branches, with multiple giant 'sausages' dangling from a single tree creating a surreal, prehistoric appearance. The flowers are deep maroon-red to brownish-red, large, bell-shaped, produced on long pendant racemes and opening at night to release a powerful, somewhat unpleasant odor that attracts bat pollinators — bats visit the flowers at night and transfer pollen between trees. The fruits require bat or other animal dispersal to germinate in the wild. The fresh fruits are toxic if consumed raw; however, the dried or roasted fruits have been used medicinally in traditional African medicine for centuries, and extracts are now investigated for pharmaceutical applications including wound healing and skin conditions. A dramatic, unmistakable specimen tree for large zone 10-11 tropical landscapes.
sources: Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Kigelia africana (Sausage Tree) cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Kigelia africana for South Florida
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Kigelia africana species profile
South African National Biodiversity Institute — Kigelia africana medicinal uses

common_name: Silver Trumpet Tree (Caribbean Trumpet Tree, Golden Tabebuia, Aurea Trumpet)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring in full sun with well-drained soil; Tabebuia aurea is one of the more cold-tolerant Tabebuia species, surviving temperatures to 25–28°F with damage to young growth. Established trees in zone 9b recover rapidly from cold damage. The tree blooms best after a dry period — withholding irrigation in late winter (February) often triggers the spectacular spring bloom. The silvery-gray, deciduous foliage provides winter interest, and the bare-branch bloom display in spring is extraordinary. Drought-tolerant once established.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, sandy to rocky, moderately fertile soil — Silver Trumpet Tree is highly drought-tolerant and performs beautifully in the sandy, alkaline soils common throughout the Gulf Coast and South Florida where many flowering trees struggle. Tabebuia aurea is a medium-sized, deciduous to semi-deciduous tree native to the dry forests and savannas of Brazil, Bolivia, Paraguay, and Argentina in the Bignoniaceae family, closely related to the widely-planted Pink Trumpet Tree (Handroanthus impetiginosus). In spring, after the dry season has caused leaf drop, the bare, silver-gray branches of Tabebuia aurea produce one of the most spectacular flowering displays of any flowering tree: masses of large, 3-inch, bright golden-yellow, trumpet-shaped flowers covering every branch simultaneously before the new leaves emerge — the tree appears as a golden cloud against the sky when viewed from a distance. The palmate, silver-green, leathery compound leaves that emerge after flowering are themselves attractive in a silvery, semi-succulent way, explaining the common name 'Silver Trumpet Tree.' Tabebuia aurea is considered one of the best-performing and most cold-tolerant of the tropical trumpet trees for zone 9 Gulf South use, and established trees in Houston, New Orleans, and adjacent areas have produced reliably spectacular spring bloom displays for decades. The national tree of Venezuela.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tabebuia aurea for Florida landscapes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tabebuia aurea for Gulf Coast zone 9
LSU AgCenter — Tropical Flowering Trees for Louisiana Landscapes
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Tabebuia aurea

common_name: Dancing Ladies Ginger (Nodding Globba, Marantina Globba, Jewel Globba)

planting.note: Plant rhizomes or container-grown clumps in spring in partial to full shade with consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil; Globba marantina cannot tolerate frost but is one of the hardier Globba species, surviving rhizome temperatures to approximately 28°F with heavy mulch in zone 9. The plant goes dormant in winter — leaves die back and rhizomes rest until spring warmth. Propagate by dividing rhizomes in spring or from bulbils (small round propagules) that form in the inflorescence instead of seeds. The arching flower stems need shade to produce their most vivid color.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil — Dancing Ladies Ginger is among the most shade-tolerant of the ornamental gingers and produces its most delicate and beautiful flower display in the filtered light under large trees. Globba marantina is a small, clump-forming, deciduous ginger native to Southeast Asia (Thailand, Malaysia, Laos, Vietnam, Indonesia) in the family Zingiberaceae, one of approximately 100 Globba species now popular in the ornamental ginger world for their delicate, dancing flower display. The plant produces stems 18–36 inches tall of broad, soft, dark-green, lance-shaped leaves with a distinctive purple blush on the undersides. In summer and fall, arching flower stalks emerge from the leaf tips bearing loosely branched inflorescences of remarkable, pendant, dancing flowers: each individual flower is about an inch long with reflexed, purple-maroon to burgundy bracts and small yellow petals that hang and dangle from thin pedicels in the slightest air movement — the massed, dancing effect of dozens of tiny, pendant purple-and-yellow flowers on arching stems is the quality that earned the genus the name 'Dancing Ladies Ginger.' A distinctive feature of Globba is the replacement of most flowers by small, round, fleshy bulbils — mini-propagules that drop and sprout new plants, forming colonies over time. One of the finest shade-tolerant gingers for zone 9 Gulf Coast woodland gardens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Globba species for Florida shade gardens
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Globba marantina cultivation
LSU AgCenter — Dancing Gingers for Gulf Coast Louisiana Shade Gardens
International Society for Horticultural Science — Globba: ornamental zingiberales

common_name: Peanut Butter Fruit (Silver-Leaved Bunchosia, Gold Cherry)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in late spring in partial shade to full sun with well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Bunchosia argentea is frost-sensitive and best suited to zone 10-11 outdoor planting or zone 9 containers. It is fast-growing and begins bearing fruit within 1–2 years from transplant. The small, yellow-orange fruits ripen throughout the warm months and are eaten fresh — they do not store or ship well. Two plants improve fruit set but a single plant often fruits acceptably. The fruit is best eaten before it becomes very soft.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Peanut Butter Fruit grows and fruits well in partial shade, making it useful for under-story tropical food garden positions under larger fruit trees. Bunchosia argentea is a fast-growing, evergreen shrub or small tree from Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Bolivia in the family Malpighiaceae, widely grown as a dooryard fruit tree throughout tropical America and increasingly available in South Florida and zone 10-11 Gulf Coast fruit collections for one of the most remarkable flavor descriptions in the tropical fruit world: the flesh of the ripe fruit has the precise taste and texture of smooth commercial peanut butter — sweet, nutty, slightly dry, and unmistakably peanut-like, despite having no botanical relationship to Arachis hypogaea (the peanut). The fruits are small (half to 1 inch), oval to oblong, and ripen from yellow-orange to orange-red, produced in clusters along the branches from the warm months. A single ripe Bunchosia argentea fruit consumed fresh is a memorable culinary experience — the resemblance to peanut butter is genuine and immediate. The plant is a rapid grower that begins bearing fruit within one to two years of planting and produces through the warm months. The silver leaf undersides (the 'argentea' — 'silvery') give the plant an attractive bi-colored appearance. An excellent, unusual addition to any tropical or subtropical home food garden.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Bunchosia argentea (Peanut Butter Fruit)
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Unusual tropical fruits: Bunchosia
Hawaii Tropical Fruit Growers Association — Bunchosia argentea cultivation notes
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Bunchosia argentea

common_name: Paradise Nut (Zabucajo, Monkey Pot, Sapucaia Nut)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in late spring in partial shade to full sun with consistently moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil; Lecythis zabucajo is frost-sensitive and suitable only for zone 10-11 outdoor planting. The tree is related to the Brazil Nut (Bertholletia excelsa) and requires similar conditions — warm, humid, and fertile. The famous 'monkey pot' fruits open with a lid (operculum) to release the seeds — the narrow pot opening allows monkeys to reach in for the nuts but traps their closed fist. The seeds (Paradise Nuts) are considered superior in flavor to Brazil Nuts.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in consistently moist, fertile, slightly acidic, deep soil; Paradise Nut Tree grows best in conditions that approximate the humid, fertile soils of its native Amazonian rainforest habitat — deep, moist, well-drained, organic-rich soil with regular rainfall. Lecythis zabucajo is a large, majestic tropical tree from the Amazon Basin (Venezuela, Guyana, Suriname, Brazil) in the Brazil-nut family (Lecythidaceae), grown for its edible seeds and as a remarkable ornamental specimen. The tree is closely related to the Brazil Nut (Bertholletia excelsa) and produces similar large, round, woody pods — but with a crucial difference: the pod of L. zabucajo has a natural operculum (lid) that opens at maturity to release the seeds, while the Brazil Nut pod does not open and must be cracked. The open-lidded pods ('monkey pots') have a narrow neck opening that is a natural primate trap: a monkey can reach in and grab a nut, but cannot withdraw its closed fist, and must release the nut or remain trapped — a phenomenon documented and described by naturalists since the 16th century. The Paradise Nuts themselves — large, brown, oily, rich — are considered by those who have tasted both to be superior to Brazil Nuts in flavor and richness. In zone 10-11 collections and botanical gardens, L. zabucajo is grown as both an edible nut tree and an ornamental specimen for its year-round beauty, including attractive pink flowers.
sources: Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Lecythis zabucajo (Paradise Nut)
Missouri Botanical Garden — Lecythis zabucajo species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Unusual nut trees for South Florida
Smithsonian Tropical Research Institute — Lecythidaceae: monkey pots and Brazil nuts
